panel.origin: The national bird of the United States — a huge raptor, unmistakable as an adult.
panel.chemistry: Adult: dark brown body with a brilliant white head and tail, heavy yellow beak. Wingspan up to ~2.3 m. Juveniles are mottled brown and often mistaken for golden eagles.
panel.mixology: Near lakes, rivers, and coasts across North America, anywhere with fish and tall nesting trees. Has recovered dramatically from near-extinction.
panel.examples: Soars on flat, plank-like wings; surprisingly weak, chirpy call (the 'eagle scream' in movies is usually a red-tailed hawk). Steals fish from ospreys.

panel.origin: The most common and widespread large hawk in North America — the default roadside hawk.
panel.chemistry: Stocky, broad-winged buteo; most adults show a brick-red tail above and a dark 'belly band' across a pale chest. Variable plumage overall.
panel.mixology: Open country, fields, deserts, and roadsides across the continent. Famously perches on highway poles scanning for prey.
panel.examples: Soars in wide circles; gives the iconic raspy, descending scream Hollywood dubs onto every eagle and hawk.

panel.origin: The fastest animal on Earth, reaching over 320 km/h in a hunting dive.
panel.chemistry: Crow-sized falcon with long pointed wings, slate-gray back, pale barred underparts, and a bold dark 'mustache' mark on the face.
panel.mixology: Cliffs, coasts, and — increasingly — city skyscrapers worldwide, where it hunts pigeons. Recovered after DDT nearly wiped it out.
panel.examples: Hunts by 'stooping' — a breathtaking high-speed dive onto birds in midair. Fast, powerful, direct flight on flickering wingbeats.

panel.origin: A large fish-eating raptor sometimes called the 'fish hawk,' found on every continent but Antarctica.
panel.chemistry: Brown above, white below, with a white head crossed by a dark eye stripe. Long, crooked wings show a dark 'wrist' patch in flight.
panel.mixology: Always near water — lakes, rivers, coasts, and reservoirs. Builds bulky stick nests on poles, platforms, and dead trees.
panel.examples: Hovers, then plunges feet-first into water to grab fish. Flies with fish held head-forward to reduce drag.

panel.origin: A large scavenger with an unfeathered red head, riding the wind on outstretched wings.
panel.chemistry: All dark with silvery flight feathers beneath, a small bare red head, and long broad wings. Holds wings in a shallow 'V' (dihedral).
panel.mixology: Across the Americas in open and wooded country. Roosts communally; follows warm air currents on migration.
panel.examples: Rocks and tilts as it soars, rarely flapping. One of the few birds with a strong sense of smell — it finds carrion by scent.

panel.origin: A medium-sized woodland hawk, the classic 'backyard bird-feeder hawk' that hunts other birds.
panel.chemistry: Slate-gray back, rusty-barred chest, long banded tail with a rounded tip, and red eyes in adults. Females noticeably larger than males.
panel.mixology: Woodlands, suburbs, and city parks across North America. Increasingly common around feeders, hunting the songbirds they attract.
panel.examples: Flies with several quick flaps then a glide; bursts through cover to ambush birds. Agile and acrobatic among trees.

panel.origin: The familiar wild duck of ponds and parks — ancestor of most domestic ducks.
panel.chemistry: Male: glossy green head, yellow bill, chestnut chest, curled black tail feathers. Female: mottled brown with an orange-and-black bill. Both show a blue wing patch.
panel.mixology: Virtually any freshwater across the Northern Hemisphere — ponds, marshes, rivers, city parks. Extremely adaptable.
panel.examples: The classic loud 'quack' is actually the female; males give a quieter rasp. 'Dabbles,' tipping bottom-up to feed.

panel.origin: The big, honking goose seen in V-formation and on countless lawns and golf courses.
panel.chemistry: Large, with a black head and neck, a white 'chinstrap' across the cheek, brown body, and pale chest.
panel.mixology: Across North America near water and grass; many populations no longer migrate and live year-round in parks and suburbs.
panel.examples: Loud, resonant honking, especially in flight. Flies in V-formations; aggressively defends nests in spring.

panel.origin: Often called the most beautiful duck in North America — a jewel of wooded swamps.
panel.chemistry: Male: iridescent green-and-purple crested head with bold white stripes, red eye, chestnut chest. Female: gray-brown with a white teardrop eye-ring.
panel.mixology: Wooded swamps, marshes, and quiet ponds with trees. Unusual among ducks for nesting in tree cavities and boxes.
panel.examples: Ducklings leap from high nest holes to the ground days after hatching. Gives a rising squeal when flushed.

panel.origin: A huge, elegant white swan with an orange bill — graceful but introduced and often aggressive.
panel.chemistry: All-white with a long curved neck, orange bill with a black knob at the base. Holds neck in a graceful S-curve.
panel.mixology: Ponds, lakes, and coastal bays; native to Eurasia but widely introduced and now feral in North America.
panel.examples: Despite the name, hisses, grunts, and snorts rather than being silent. Aggressively defends territory, even against people.
panel.flag: Mute swans can be aggressive near nests — admire them from a respectful distance.

panel.origin: An iconic diving bird of northern lakes, famous for its haunting, echoing call.
panel.chemistry: Breeding adult: black head, blood-red eye, checkerboard black-and-white back, banded neck collar. Heavy, dagger-like bill.
panel.mixology: Clear northern lakes in summer; coasts and large lakes in winter (in plainer gray plumage). A symbol of northern wilderness.
panel.examples: Its wailing, yodeling, and laughing calls carry across lakes at night. Dives deep to catch fish; nearly helpless on land.

panel.origin: The classic harbinger of spring — a familiar thrush hopping across lawns.
panel.chemistry: Gray-brown above with a warm brick-orange breast, dark head, white throat streaks, and a yellow bill.
panel.mixology: Lawns, gardens, parks, and woodlands across North America. Among the first birds singing before dawn.
panel.examples: Cocks its head to 'listen' for worms, then runs and stops across the grass. Sings a cheerful, caroling 'cheerily, cheer-up'.

panel.origin: A beloved year-round backyard bird — the bright red 'redbird' of the eastern US.
panel.chemistry: Male: brilliant red all over with a black face mask and a pointed crest. Female: warm tan with red tinges, same crest and mask.
panel.mixology: Woodland edges, gardens, and shrubby backyards across the eastern and central US. A common, welcome feeder visitor.
panel.examples: Both sexes sing a clear, whistled 'birdy-birdy-birdy' or 'cheer-cheer-cheer.' Doesn't migrate, brightening winter feeders.

panel.origin: A small, vivid thrush and a classic symbol of happiness, saved by nest-box programs.
panel.chemistry: Male: deep blue above with a rusty-orange throat and chest, white belly. Female: grayer with blue-tinged wings and tail.
panel.mixology: Open country with scattered trees — meadows, orchards, and roadsides — in the eastern US. Nests in cavities and boxes.
panel.examples: Perches low and drops to catch insects. Gives a soft, warbling 'chur-lee' call. Recovered thanks to widespread nest boxes.

panel.origin: The bright 'wild canary' that brightens summer fields and thistle feeders.
panel.chemistry: Breeding male: brilliant lemon-yellow with a black cap and black-and-white wings. Female and winter birds: dull olive-yellow.
panel.mixology: Weedy fields, gardens, and roadsides across North America. A late nester, timed to thistle and sunflower seeds.
panel.examples: Bounces through the air in deep undulations, calling 'po-ta-to-chip.' Almost entirely vegetarian, eating seeds.

panel.origin: A widespread, streaky brown sparrow with one of the most variable, musical songs.
panel.chemistry: Brown and gray with heavy dark streaks converging into a central spot on the chest. Long, rounded tail it pumps in flight.
panel.mixology: Brushy fields, marsh edges, gardens, and thickets across most of North America. One of the continent's most common sparrows.
panel.examples: Sings a bright, complex song that varies by region — a few clear notes followed by a trill. Pumps its tail as it flies.

panel.origin: An abundant marsh blackbird whose males flash scarlet shoulder patches.
panel.chemistry: Male: glossy black with bright red-and-yellow shoulder patches (epaulets). Female: heavily streaked brown, looking like a large sparrow.
panel.mixology: Marshes, wet fields, and roadside ditches across North America — one of the continent's most numerous birds.
panel.examples: Males sing a gurgling 'conk-la-ree!' from cattails and fiercely defend territory, even dive-bombing intruders.

panel.origin: A flame-orange songbird that weaves a hanging, sock-like nest.
panel.chemistry: Male: brilliant orange and black with white wing bars. Female: yellow-orange and grayer. Slender, pointed bill.
panel.mixology: Open woodlands, parks, and leafy suburbs in the eastern US in summer; winters in the tropics.
panel.examples: Gives a rich, flutelike whistle from the treetops. Sips nectar and fruit — attracted to orange halves and jelly feeders.

panel.origin: The familiar all-black, highly intelligent bird with the classic 'caw.'
panel.chemistry: Entirely glossy black, including the bill and legs; fan-shaped tail in flight. Smaller than a raven, with a straighter bill.
panel.mixology: Nearly everywhere in North America — fields, towns, woods, and dumps. Gathers in huge winter roosts.
panel.examples: Says a clear 'caw-caw.' Remarkably smart: uses tools, recognizes human faces, and remembers them for years.

panel.origin: A large, shaggy, supremely intelligent black bird of wild and mountainous country.
panel.chemistry: Bigger and bulkier than a crow, with a heavy bill, shaggy throat feathers, and a wedge-shaped (diamond) tail in flight.
panel.mixology: Mountains, forests, deserts, and coasts across the Northern Hemisphere. Returning to many areas it was once driven from.
panel.examples: Gives a deep, croaking 'gronk' rather than a caw. Soars and tumbles in acrobatic flight; among the smartest of all birds.

panel.origin: A bold, noisy, crested blue bird that dominates eastern feeders.
panel.chemistry: Bright blue above with white underparts, a blue crest, black necklace, and white-and-black barred wings and tail.
panel.mixology: Oak and mixed woods, parks, and suburbs in the eastern and central US. Plants oak forests by caching acorns.
panel.examples: Loud and varied: harsh 'jay-jay' screams, plus eerily accurate imitations of hawk calls to scare other birds off feeders.

panel.origin: A flashy, long-tailed black-and-white relative of crows in the open West.
panel.chemistry: Bold black-and-white pattern with iridescent blue-green wings and an extremely long, tapering tail.
panel.mixology: Open country, ranchland, and streamside thickets in the western US and Canada. Often seen near livestock.
panel.examples: Noisy, chattering calls. Builds huge domed stick nests. Curious and intelligent, like its crow and jay relatives.

panel.origin: The smallest and most common North American woodpecker — a tiny feeder regular.
panel.chemistry: Black-and-white with a checkered back, white belly, and (in males) a small red patch on the back of the head. Tiny, stubby bill.
panel.mixology: Woodlands, parks, and backyards across most of North America. Readily visits suet feeders.
panel.examples: Gives a soft 'pik' and a descending whinny. Taps lightly on small branches; nearly identical to the larger Hairy Woodpecker.

panel.origin: A spectacular crow-sized woodpecker with a flaming red crest — the 'Woody Woodpecker' bird.
panel.chemistry: Big and black with a bright red triangular crest, white neck stripes, and white wing flashes in flight.
panel.mixology: Mature forests with large dead trees across much of North America. Increasingly seen in wooded suburbs.
panel.examples: Loud, ringing, irregular call and powerful drumming. Chisels large rectangular holes in trees hunting carpenter ants.

panel.origin: An unusual brown woodpecker that feeds on the ground, eating ants.
panel.chemistry: Brown with black bars and spots, a black chest crescent, and a flash of yellow or red under the wings and tail. White rump in flight.
panel.mixology: Open woods, parks, and lawns across North America. Often seen hopping on the ground rather than on tree trunks.
panel.examples: Gives a loud, rolling 'wick-wick-wick' and a sharp 'klee-yer.' Drums on metal gutters in spring to claim territory.

panel.origin: A common eastern woodpecker, confusingly named for a faint belly tinge, not its bold red cap.
panel.chemistry: Pale gray face and underparts with a black-and-white 'zebra' back and a red crown and nape (the belly red is hard to see).
panel.mixology: Woodlands, parks, and suburbs in the eastern US, expanding northward. A frequent feeder visitor.
panel.examples: Gives a rolling 'churr' call. Stores food in bark crevices; comes readily to suet and seed feeders.

panel.origin: A tall, stately wading bird — the largest heron in North America.
panel.chemistry: Blue-gray with a long neck, dagger bill, black eye plume, and long legs. Stands over a meter tall; flies with neck folded into an S.
panel.mixology: Marshes, shorelines, rivers, and ponds across North America. Nests colonially in treetop 'rookeries.'
panel.examples: Stalks slowly in shallow water, then spears fish with a lightning strike. Gives a harsh, croaking squawk when flushed.

panel.origin: An enormous white waterbird with one of the largest wingspans on the continent.
panel.chemistry: Bright white with black wingtips, a huge orange bill and throat pouch. Wingspan up to ~2.7 m.
panel.mixology: Large lakes and marshes in the interior West in summer; southern coasts in winter. Breeds in colonies on isolated islands.
panel.examples: Unlike brown pelicans, it doesn't dive — groups herd fish cooperatively at the surface and scoop them up. Soars in graceful formations.

panel.origin: The common 'seagull' of parking lots, parks, and lakes far from any sea.
panel.chemistry: White with a gray back, black wingtips, yellow legs, and a yellow bill with a distinct black ring near the tip.
panel.mixology: Lakes, rivers, fields, and urban areas across North America — often nowhere near the ocean.
panel.examples: Bold and adaptable, scavenging fries and scraps. Gives the classic squealing gull calls. Takes years to reach adult plumage.

panel.origin: A tall gray crane famous for spectacular migrations and bugling calls.
panel.chemistry: Tall and gray with a red crown, long neck and legs, and a bustle of feathers over the rump. Flies with neck outstretched.
panel.mixology: Marshes, prairies, and fields. Gathers by the hundreds of thousands at migration stopovers like Nebraska's Platte River.
panel.examples: Gives a loud, rolling, bugling call audible for miles. Performs leaping, bowing courtship dances. Flies with neck straight, unlike herons.

panel.origin: A stocky, big-headed bird that dives headfirst for fish.
panel.chemistry: Blue-gray with a shaggy crest, white collar, and a heavy bill. Females have an extra rusty band across the belly.
panel.mixology: Along rivers, lakes, and coasts across North America. Nests in burrows dug into earthen banks.
panel.examples: Gives a loud, dry rattle as it flies along the water. Hovers, then plunges headfirst to catch fish.

panel.origin: A powerful, wide-ranging owl with prominent ear tufts — the classic 'hoot owl.'
panel.chemistry: Large and bulky with feathered 'horns' (ear tufts), a white throat patch, yellow eyes, and mottled gray-brown camouflage.
panel.mixology: Forests, deserts, city parks — nearly every habitat across the Americas. One of the most adaptable owls.
panel.examples: Gives a deep, rhythmic 'hoo-h'HOO-hoo-hoo.' A fierce nocturnal hunter that even takes skunks and other raptors.

panel.origin: A round-headed, dark-eyed owl of wet woodlands, known for its 'who-cooks-for-you' call.
panel.chemistry: Stocky and gray-brown with no ear tufts, dark brown eyes (most owls have yellow), and vertical belly streaks over horizontal chest bars.
panel.mixology: Mature, often swampy forests in the eastern US, expanding into the Pacific Northwest. Roosts quietly by day.
panel.examples: Famous hooting cadence sounds like 'Who cooks for you? Who cooks for you-all?' Active at dusk and on cloudy days.

panel.origin: A pale, ghostly, heart-faced owl found on nearly every continent.
panel.chemistry: Pale golden-buff above, white below, with a distinctive white heart-shaped facial disc and dark eyes. Long legs.
panel.mixology: Open country, farmland, and grasslands worldwide. Nests in barns, hollow trees, and nest boxes.
panel.examples: Gives an eerie, raspy shriek rather than a hoot. Hunts low over fields, locating rodents by sound with extraordinary hearing.

panel.origin: A tiny, well-camouflaged owl with a wavering, whinnying call — not a screech.
panel.chemistry: Small with ear tufts and yellow eyes; comes in gray or rusty-red color forms. Superbly camouflaged against bark.
panel.mixology: Wooded suburbs, parks, and forests in the eastern US. Roosts in tree cavities and nest boxes, often in plain sight.
panel.examples: Gives a soft, descending whinny and an even trill — not an actual screech. Strictly nocturnal and easily overlooked.

panel.origin: A large white owl of the Arctic, made famous as Harry Potter's Hedwig.
panel.chemistry: Big and rounded with no ear tufts; males nearly pure white, females and young flecked with dark bars. Bright yellow eyes.
panel.mixology: Breeds on the open Arctic tundra; some winters move south into fields, airports, and shorelines ('irruptions').
panel.examples: Largely silent in winter. Hunts by day as well as night, unusual for an owl — adapted to the round-the-clock Arctic summer.

panel.origin: A tiny, fearless, endlessly cheerful feeder bird that often comes to the hand.
panel.chemistry: Round little bird with a black cap and bib, white cheeks, gray back, and buffy sides. Big head, small body.
panel.mixology: Woods, parks, and backyards across the northern US and Canada. A constant, tame presence at feeders.
panel.examples: Says its own name: 'chick-a-dee-dee-dee,' adding more 'dees' when alarmed. Also whistles a clear 'fee-bee.'

panel.origin: The ubiquitous little brown bird of cities and farms worldwide — introduced from Europe.
panel.chemistry: Male: gray crown, black bib, chestnut nape, streaky brown back. Female: plain brown with a pale eyebrow stripe.
panel.mixology: Cities, farms, and buildings on every inhabited continent. Thrives around human structures and activity.
panel.examples: Gives monotonous chirps and cheeps. Highly social, nesting in building crevices; an aggressive competitor with native birds.

panel.origin: A slim, soft-gray dove whose mournful cooing is one of the most familiar bird sounds.
panel.chemistry: Slender and gray-tan with black spots on the wings, a long pointed tail with white edges, and a small head.
panel.mixology: Open country, farms, and suburbs across North America — one of the most abundant and widespread birds.
panel.examples: Its low, sad 'coo-OO-oo-oo-oo' is often mistaken for an owl. Wings whistle sharply on takeoff.

panel.origin: A tiny, dazzling hummingbird — the common breeding hummingbird of eastern North America.
panel.chemistry: Iridescent green above, pale below; males flash a brilliant ruby-red throat that can look black in poor light. Needle-like bill.
panel.mixology: Gardens, woodland edges, and feeders in the eastern US in summer. Migrates astonishingly far for its size, crossing the Gulf of Mexico.
panel.examples: Hovers and even flies backward, wings humming. Fiercely territorial at flowers and nectar feeders despite its tiny size.

panel.origin: A compact bird famous for walking headfirst down tree trunks.
panel.chemistry: Blue-gray back, white face and underparts, black cap, and a long straight bill. Stubby tail, big head.
panel.mixology: Mature woods, parks, and wooded backyards across North America. A regular at seed and suet feeders.
panel.examples: Gives a nasal 'yank-yank.' Creeps down trunks head-first, a trick that lets it spot insects other birds miss.

panel.origin: A small, neat sparrow nicknamed the 'snowbird' for arriving with winter.
panel.chemistry: Slate-gray or brown above with a crisp white belly, pink bill, and white outer tail feathers that flash in flight.
panel.mixology: Breeds in northern and mountain forests; floods into backyards and feeders across the US in winter.
panel.examples: Gives a musical trill and sharp smacking call notes. Forages on the ground, flashing white tail feathers as it flushes.

BIRDS OF PREY.en: That fierce 'eagle scream' in nearly every movie is a lie — it's almost always a Red-tailed Hawk dubbed in, because the Bald Eagle's real call is a weak, chirpy whistle.
BIRDS OF PREY.es: That fierce 'eagle scream' in nearly every movie is a lie — it's almost always a Red-tailed Hawk dubbed in, because the Bald Eagle's real call is a weak, chirpy whistle.
BIRDS OF PREY.fr: That fierce 'eagle scream' in nearly every movie is a lie — it's almost always a Red-tailed Hawk dubbed in, because the Bald Eagle's real call is a weak, chirpy whistle.
BIRDS OF PREY.it: That fierce 'eagle scream' in nearly every movie is a lie — it's almost always a Red-tailed Hawk dubbed in, because the Bald Eagle's real call is a weak, chirpy whistle.

WATERFOWL.en: The famous duck 'quack' belongs almost entirely to the female Mallard — males make a much quieter, raspier sound, and most other duck species don't quack at all.
WATERFOWL.es: The famous duck 'quack' belongs almost entirely to the female Mallard — males make a much quieter, raspier sound, and most other duck species don't quack at all.
WATERFOWL.fr: The famous duck 'quack' belongs almost entirely to the female Mallard — males make a much quieter, raspier sound, and most other duck species don't quack at all.
WATERFOWL.it: The famous duck 'quack' belongs almost entirely to the female Mallard — males make a much quieter, raspier sound, and most other duck species don't quack at all.

SONGBIRDS.en: A male songbird's brain literally grows new song-learning neurons each breeding season — birdsong has taught scientists much of what we know about how brains learn.
SONGBIRDS.es: A male songbird's brain literally grows new song-learning neurons each breeding season — birdsong has taught scientists much of what we know about how brains learn.
SONGBIRDS.fr: A male songbird's brain literally grows new song-learning neurons each breeding season — birdsong has taught scientists much of what we know about how brains learn.
SONGBIRDS.it: A male songbird's brain literally grows new song-learning neurons each breeding season — birdsong has taught scientists much of what we know about how brains learn.

CROWS & JAYS.en: Crows can recognize and remember individual human faces for years, even teaching their offspring which people to distrust — they hold genuine grudges.
CROWS & JAYS.es: Crows can recognize and remember individual human faces for years, even teaching their offspring which people to distrust — they hold genuine grudges.
CROWS & JAYS.fr: Crows can recognize and remember individual human faces for years, even teaching their offspring which people to distrust — they hold genuine grudges.
CROWS & JAYS.it: Crows can recognize and remember individual human faces for years, even teaching their offspring which people to distrust — they hold genuine grudges.

WOODPECKERS.en: A woodpecker hammers up to 20 times a second and decelerates at over 1,000 g's per peck, yet never gets a concussion thanks to a built-in shock-absorbing skull.
WOODPECKERS.es: A woodpecker hammers up to 20 times a second and decelerates at over 1,000 g's per peck, yet never gets a concussion thanks to a built-in shock-absorbing skull.
WOODPECKERS.fr: A woodpecker hammers up to 20 times a second and decelerates at over 1,000 g's per peck, yet never gets a concussion thanks to a built-in shock-absorbing skull.
WOODPECKERS.it: A woodpecker hammers up to 20 times a second and decelerates at over 1,000 g's per peck, yet never gets a concussion thanks to a built-in shock-absorbing skull.

SHORE & WATER.en: A Great Blue Heron hunts by standing motionless for minutes, then striking faster than you can blink — its S-curved neck snaps straight like a spring-loaded spear.
SHORE & WATER.es: A Great Blue Heron hunts by standing motionless for minutes, then striking faster than you can blink — its S-curved neck snaps straight like a spring-loaded spear.
SHORE & WATER.fr: A Great Blue Heron hunts by standing motionless for minutes, then striking faster than you can blink — its S-curved neck snaps straight like a spring-loaded spear.
SHORE & WATER.it: A Great Blue Heron hunts by standing motionless for minutes, then striking faster than you can blink — its S-curved neck snaps straight like a spring-loaded spear.

OWLS.en: An owl's facial disc works like a satellite dish, funneling sound to ears set at different heights so it can pinpoint a mouse under snow in total darkness — by hearing alone.
OWLS.es: An owl's facial disc works like a satellite dish, funneling sound to ears set at different heights so it can pinpoint a mouse under snow in total darkness — by hearing alone.
OWLS.fr: An owl's facial disc works like a satellite dish, funneling sound to ears set at different heights so it can pinpoint a mouse under snow in total darkness — by hearing alone.
OWLS.it: An owl's facial disc works like a satellite dish, funneling sound to ears set at different heights so it can pinpoint a mouse under snow in total darkness — by hearing alone.

BACKYARD BIRDS.en: A chickadee can lower its body temperature at night to save energy and remembers thousands of separate food-cache locations — its brain actually grows in fall to manage them.
BACKYARD BIRDS.es: A chickadee can lower its body temperature at night to save energy and remembers thousands of separate food-cache locations — its brain actually grows in fall to manage them.
BACKYARD BIRDS.fr: A chickadee can lower its body temperature at night to save energy and remembers thousands of separate food-cache locations — its brain actually grows in fall to manage them.
BACKYARD BIRDS.it: A chickadee can lower its body temperature at night to save energy and remembers thousands of separate food-cache locations — its brain actually grows in fall to manage them.
